methods: 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Test chemicals The ICH guideline lists 32 pharmaceutical drugs as reference compounds to validate alternative assays, namely acitretin, aspirin, bosentan, busulfan, carbamazepine, cetirizine, cisplatin, cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, dabrafenib, dasatinib, fluconazole, fluorouracil, hydroxyurea, iburutinib, ibuprofen, imatinib, isotretinoin, methotrexate, pazopanib, phenytoin, pomalidomide, ribavirin, saxagliptin, tacrolimus, thalidomide, topiramate, tretinoin, trimethadione, valproic acid, vildagliptin, and vismodegib ( ICH, 2020 ). Specific metabolites are also described for 4 of these drugs, as they may be involved in the embryotoxic actions of the drugs, specifically, salicylic acid for aspirin, phosphoramide mustard and acrolein for cyclophosphamide, triazole carboxamide for ribavirin, and dimethadione for trimethadione. Note that the contribution of triazole carboxamide to the embryotoxic action is unknown, as indicated in the ICH guideline. The present study also examined 4-hydroperoxy cyclophosphamide in relation to cyclophosphamide, although it is not mentioned in the ICH guideline. This is because 4-hydroperoxy cyclophosphamide spontaneously breaks down in aqueous solutions into phosphoramide mustard and acrolein ( Supplementary Figure S1 ), which may better emulate the in vivo metabolic transformation of cyclophosphamide in the in vitro setting ( Slott and Hales, 1988 ). All of these chemicals are listed in Table 1 with the information on their commercial source and stock preparation. All stocks were stored at −20°C, and defrosted for further dilutions immediately before the experiments were performed ( section 2.3 ). 2.2. Cell culture and 3D gastruloid generation Maintenance culture and generation of 3D gastruloids of mouse P19C5 stem cells were conducted, according to the method described previously ( Warkus and Marikawa, 2017 ) with a slight modification. The culture medium used for the present study consisted of ‘MEM α, no nucleosides’ (catalog number 12561, Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) instead of the previous ‘MEM α, nucleosides, GlutaMAX Supplement’ (catalog number 32571, Gibco). The purpose of this modification was to allow the detection of toxicity caused by a drug that interferes with de novo nucleotide synthesis (e.g., methotrexate) and also to avoid the unexpected influence of the artificial substance GlutaMAX. Pilot studies confirmed that these changes had no significant effect on cell proliferation or gastruloid formation in comparison with the previous culture medium. The procedure of 3D gastruloid generation by hanging drop culture is depicted in Figure 1A . 2.3. Chemical treatment In each set of experiment, P19C5 cell aggregates were treated with a test chemical at varying concentrations (2-fold serial dilutions), including a negative control (no test chemical), according to the method described previously ( Warkus and Marikawa, 2017 ). Briefly, dissociated cells were suspended at a density of 10 cells/μL in the culture medium containing 1% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) with a specific concentration of the test chemical ( Figure 1A ). The inclusion of 1% DMSO is necessary to induce gastruloid formation and is not toxic to P19C5 cells ( Marikawa et al., 2009 ; Marikawa and Lau, 2014 ). Drops (20 μL each) of cell suspension were spotted on the inner surface of Petri dish lids (16 drops per dish for each treatment) to create cell aggregates by hanging drop culture ( Figure 1A ). Cell aggregates were cultured in hanging drops for 4 days, followed by morphological analysis ( section 2.4 ). The range of test concentrations was selected for each chemical to include both a no adverse effect concentration and an adverse effect concentration based on a series of pilot studies ( section 2.5 ). For 6 chemicals (pomalidomide, saxagliptin, triazole carboxamide, thalidomide, trimethadione, and vildagliptin), the highest concentration was set by the limit of the solubility in solvent or culture medium, even though it did not cause an adverse effect. For each test chemical, three or more sets of experiments were performed on different days as biological replicates. 2.4. Morphological analysis The morphology of chemical-treated cell aggregates, or gastruloids, was analyzed, according to the method described previously ( Warkus and Marikawa, 2017 ). Briefly, after 4 days of culture, gastruloids were removed from hanging drops and photographed ( Figure 1B ). The images were opened in the ImageJ program ( Schneider et al., 2012 ), and morphological parameters were measured by manually tracing the circumference of individual gastruloids with the polygon selection tool. Three parameters were used to assess gastruloid morphology: area, elongation distortion index (EDI), and aspect ratio (AR). The area served as a proxy for the overall size of gastruloids. EDI (= 1/circularity−1; Marikawa et al., 2009 ) and AR are shape descriptors to quantify the extent of elongation, with EDI and AR increasing when gastruloids are more elongated. For each set of experiment, the area, EDI and adjusted AR (= AR-1) values were normalized against the average values of control gastruloids, and were expressed as relative area, relative EDI, and relative AR, respectively. These relative values are presented as percentages, such that the average of control gastruloids is 100%. Data from all replicates were compiled, processed for statistical analysis ( section 2.5 ), and presented in figures as mean ± 95% confidence intervals (CI). Importantly, for the quality assurance of the assay, only those sets of experiments with control gastruloids exhibiting robust growth and elongation were deemed valid and included for further analyses. Specifically, the average area, EDI, and AR of control gastruloids should be larger than 50,000 μm 2 , 0.4, and 2.2, respectively. This is to ensure that chemical effects were tested on robustly developing gastruloids. 2.5. Statistical analysis and adverse effect criteria An adverse effect of a chemical treatment was determined in two steps, according to the procedure described previously ( Warkus and Marikawa, 2017 ). First, a two-sample t-test was performed between the control and chemical-treated groups to verify that the observed change in relative area, EDI, or AR was statistically significant ( p <0.01). Then, the chemical treatment was defined as ‘adverse’ when it fulfilled either of the following conditions: (1) a reduction in the relative area by more than 20%, and (2) a decrease or increase in the relative EDI or relative AR by more than 40%. The highest concentration with no adverse effect was defined as the in vitro no-observed-adverse-effect-level (NOAEL), and the lowest concentration with an adverse effect was defined as the in vitro lowest-observed-adverse-effect-level (LOAEL) for each chemical. 2.6. Comparisons between in vitro and in vivo adverse effect concentrations The ICH guideline provides in vivo plasma levels of chemicals as the maximum concentration (C max ) as well as the area under the curve (AUC; measured as AUC 0–24 h or AUC 0-inf in most cases). For the present study, AUC was divided by 24 to calculate the average plasma concentration over 24 h (C ave ) ( Smith et al., 2010 ). For example, AUC for acitretin NOAEL is 20.2 μM*h, so C ave is 0.84 μM (= 20.2/24). AUC for hydroxyurea is not provided in the ICH guideline, so C ave was estimated based on its C max and half-life (15 min in rats; ICH, 2020 ), as described in Supplementary Figure S2 . The in vitro NOAEL and LOAEL from the gastruloid assay were compared with the in vivo values in rats (or mice in the case of cytarabine) for both C max and C ave to evaluate similarities between the in vitro and in vivo assay results. Note that cytarabine is the only reference drug for which the ICH guideline provides in vitro plasma concentrations in rats as well as in mice. Because P19C5 cells are of mouse origin, the in vivo NOEAL and LOAEL in mice were compared for cytarabine. The ICH reference drugs are categorized into two groups according to the availability of NOAEL and LOAEL information. The first group is provided with both NOAEL and LOAEL ( Table 2 ), whereas the second group is provided only with NOAEL or LOAEL ( Table 3 ). The first group with both NOAEL and LOAEL allows the most rigorous assessment of similarity in sensitivity between the in vitro and in vivo assays. For these chemicals, the concentration range between NOAEL and LOAEL of the gastruloid assay was compared with that of the in vivo, and the comparison was classified as a ‘match’ when the two ranges overlapped with each other within an 8-fold concentration margin ( Figure 1C ). The extent of the match was further graded as ++++, +++, ++, and +, when they overlapped within a 1-fold, 2-fold, 4-fold, and 8-fold concentration margin, respectively ( Figure 1C ). By contrast, the second group of drugs with only NOAEL or LOAEL is not sufficient to evaluate the sensitivity of the assay as rigorously as the first group. Nonetheless, they can still provide insights on the potential capability and limitation of alternative assays. For the drugs with only in vivo LOAEL, the gastrulation assay was considered ‘not contradictory’ when the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range overlapped with the in vivo LOAEL (C max or C ave ) within an 8-fold concentration margin. For the drugs with only in vivo NOAEL, the gastruloid assay was considered ‘not contradictory’ when the in vitro NOAEL was higher than the in vivo NOAEL.
results: 3. Results 3.1. Determination of in vitro NOAEL and LOAEL using the gastruloid assay We first determined the in vitro NOAEL and LOAEL of the reference chemicals listed in the ICH guideline, using the P19C5 gastruloid assay. A substantial change in the morphological parameters of gastruloids (area, EDI, and AR) was scored as the adverse effect of a chemical treatment, as defined in Materials and methods (2.5). Representative images of chemical-treated gastruloids are shown in Figure 1B and Supplementary Figure S3 . Through the testing of 2-fold dilution series, both NOAEL and LOAEL were identified for 32 out of the 38 chemicals examined ( Figure 2 ). Additionally, NOAEL and LOAEL were determined for an equimolar mix of phosphoramide mustard and acrolein, both of which are the embryotoxic metabolites of cyclophosphamide ( Supplementary Figure S1 ). No adverse effect was found for 6 chemicals, namely pomalidomide, triazole carboxamide, saxagliptin, thalidomide, trimethadione, and vildagliption, even at the highest concentration tested. For these chemicals, NOAEL was assigned to the highest concentration tested, and LOAEL was not determined ( Tables 2 , 3 ). 3.2. Validation of the gastruloid assay based on reference drugs that have both NOAEL and LOAEL information Twenty-four pharmaceutical drugs in the ICH guideline are provided with both NOAEL and LOAEL plasma concentrations in rodents ( Table 2 ). For two of these drugs (aspirin and trimethadione), NOAEL and LOAEL are also provided for their metabolites (salicylic acid and dimethadione, respectively). Having both NOAEL and LOAEL, all of these chemicals are most useful to validate how close the sensitivity of in vitro assays is to that of the in vivo. We compared the NOAEL to LOAEL concentration range of the gastruloid assay with that of the in vivo data for C max and C ave (= AUC/24; see 2.6 ) ( Figure 3 ). When the in vitro range overlapped with the in vivo range (either C max or C ave ) within an 8-fold concentration margin, the comparison was deemed a ‘match’, as defined in Materials and methods (2.6) and as depicted in Figure 1C . The in vitro and in vivo ranges matched for 18 out of the 24 pharmaceutical drugs, namely aspirin, bosentan, carbamazepine, cetirizine, cisplatin, cytarabine, dabrafenib, fluconazole, fluorouracil, hydroxyurea, ibrutinib, ibuprofen, imatinib, pazopanib, phenytoin, topiramate, trimethadione, and valproic acid ( Figure 3 , Table 2 ). Note that trimethadione was considered a match (in spite of the absence of in vitro LOAEL), because its metabolite dimethadione was a match (++ for C max , +++ for C ave ). Also, the extent of the match was higher for salicylic acid (++++ for C max , ++ for C ave ) than aspirin (++ for C max , unmatch for C ave ). These two examples implicate that the gastruloid assay may be more effective to detect embryotoxic metabolites than their precursors, as expected for in vitro assays that do not recapitulate maternal metabolisms. There appeared to be no clear difference between C max and C ave in terms of the extent of the match of the gastruloid assay: some drugs (aspirin [including salicylic acid], bosentan, cisplatin, dabrafenib, and ibrutinib) matched better for C max , whereas other drugs (cetirizine, cytarabine, fluorouracil, hydroxyurea, ibuprofen, pazopanib, and trimethadione [specifically dimethadione]) matched better for C ave ( Table 2 ). Three drugs of the latter group, namely cytarabine, fluorouracil, and hydroxyurea, matched only for C ave but not for C max . Interestingly, these three drugs are specifically noted in the ICH guideline as those having very short half-life ( ICH, 2020 ), which implicates that C ave , rather than C max , should be examined by the gastruloid assay for drugs that are metabolized and/or excreted rapidly in vivo. The in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range did not match the in vivo range for 6 drugs (neither C max nor C ave ) ( Table 2 ). Among these drugs, the in vitro range was too low compared to the in vivo for acitretin, isotretinoin, and tretinoin. Interestingly, all of these drugs are retinoids ( Scheinfeld, 2006 ), implicating that the gastruloid assay may be excessively sensitive to the activation of retinoic acid signaling. By contrast, the gastruloid assay may be unable to detect the embryotoxicity of ribavirin, tacrolimus, and thalidomide in a sensitive manner, because the in vitro LOAEL was either too high compared to the in vivo LOAEL (for ribavirin and tacrolimus) or not identified (for thalidomide) ( Figure 2 ). Note that triazole carboxamide is mentioned in the ICH guideline as a metabolite of ribavirin, even though its involvement in the embryotoxicity is unknown ( ICH, 2020 ). Triazole carboxamide did not show an adverse effect on gastruloids even at the highest concentration tested (100 μM), while the in vitro LOAEL of ribavirin was 16 μM ( Figure 2 ). 3.3. Comparison between in vitro and in vivo data for reference drugs that have only LOAEL or NOAEL information Six pharmaceutical drugs in the ICH guideline (busulfan, cyclophosphamide, dasatinib, methotrexate, pomalidomide, and vismodegib) are provided with only LOAEL, whereas 2 drugs (saxagliptin and vildagliptin) are provided with only NOAEL ( Table 3 ). These chemicals are not sufficient to rigorously validate the sensitivity of the gastruloid assay. Nevertheless, they may still be useful to gauge the potential capability and limitation of in vitro assays. For busulfan, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range overlapped with the in vivo LOAEL C max within an 8-fold concentration margin ( Figure 4 ), implicating that the sensitivity of the gastruloid assay may potentially be in line with that of the in vivo. For cyclophosphamide, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range (500–1000 μM) was more than 30-fold higher than the in vivo LOAEL C max (16 μM) and 850-fold higher than the LOAEL C ave (0.58 μM) ( Figure 4 , Table 3 ), indicating that gastruloids were not impaired by cyclophosphamide at in vivo-relevant concentrations. However, for the embryotoxic metabolites of cyclophosphamide, namely phosphoramide mustard and acrolein, the gastruloid assay exhibited adverse effects at concentrations that were close to their in vivo LOAEL C max (2.49 μM). Specifically, the in vitro LOAELs for phosphoramide mustard, acrolein, and their equimolar mix were 20 μM, 5 μM, and 2.5 μM, respectively. For acrolein and the equimolar mix, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range also overlapped with the in vivo LOAEL C ave (0.4 μM) within an 8-fold concentration margin ( Figure 4 ). Notably, 4-hydroperoxy cyclophosphamide, which is an activated form of cyclophosphamide that spontaneously breaks into phosphoramide mustard and acrolein in aqueous solution ( Supplementary Figure S1 ), impaired gastruloids at the in vitro LOAEL of 0.8 μM ( Figure 2 ). Thus, gastruloids were more sensitively affected by the metabolites of cyclophosphamide, further supporting the notion that the gastruloid assay is suited to detect embryotoxic metabolites than their precursors. The in vitro values were not contradictory to the in vivo data for dasatinib, methotrexate, and vismodegib. Specifically, for dasatinib, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range (12.5–25 nM) overlapped with the in vivo LOAEL C max (43 nM) and C ave (9.0 nM) within a 2-fold concentration margin ( Figure 4 ). For methotrexate, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range (20–40 nM) was lower than the in vivo LOAEL C max (460 nM) and overlapped with C ave (6.1 nM) within a 4-fold margin ( Figure 4 ). For vismodegib, the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL range (10–20 μM) overlapped with the in vivo LOAEL C max (17 μM) and C ave (5.0 μM) within a 4-fold concentration margin ( Figure 4 ). By contrast, for pomalidomide, no adverse effect was identified with the gastruloid assay even at the highest concentration tested (400 μM), which was more than 32-fold higher than the in vivo LOAEL C max (9.9 μM) and C ave (5.2 μM) ( Figure 4 ). This implicates that the gastruloid assay does not detect the embryotoxicity of pomalidomide. Saxagliptin and vildagliptin are described in the ICH guideline as negative control reference compounds, and are provided with only NOAEL. The in vitro NOAEL was 2000 μM for saxagliptin, which is higher than the in vivo NOAEL C max (750 μM) and C ave (81 μM). For vildagliptin, the in vitro NOAEL was 1000 μM, which is markedly higher than the in vivo NOAEL C ave (33 μM; C max is not provided in the ICH guideline). These results suggest that the gastruloid assay is consistent with the in vivo data for these negative control drugs.
discussion: 4. Discussion In the present study, we used the reference chemicals of the ICH S5(R3) Step 5 guideline ( ICH, 2020 ) to validate a mouse gastruloid assay as an NAM for DART assessment. The ICH guideline provides the in vivo exposure data in rodents, namely NOAEL and LOAEL plasma concentrations, for 24 pharmaceutical drugs. These reference drugs with both the NOAEL and LOAEL information are particularly useful to conduct rigorous ‘exposure-based validation’ ( Daston et al., 2010 , 2014 ), because toxicity (or lack thereof) is dependent on concentrations, as underscored by a basic principle of toxicology: the dose makes the poison. Here, we showed that the sensitivity (i.e., the NOAEL to LOAEL concentration range) of the gastruloid assay was comparable to that in rodents within an 8-fold concentration margin for 18 of the 24 reference drugs (75%). The ICH guideline also provides additional 8 reference drugs with only the LOAEL or NOAEL data in rodents. These reference drugs may not be sufficient to validate the sensitivity of in vitro assays in a rigorous manner. Nonetheless, for 7 of these 8 drugs, the NOAEL and LOAEL values of the gastruloid assay were not contradictory to the in vivo data. Thus, the present study demonstrated that the gastruloid assay of mouse P19C5 pluripotent stem cells is an effective NAM overall. Importantly, a single in vitro platform cannot sufficiently assess the drug safety on all stages and aspects of development and reproduction. It is vital to assemble a collection of qualified alternative assays that can complement each other’s weakness to detect a wide variety of DART ( Marikawa, 2022 ). To identify the strength and weakness of each assay, rigorous validation must be done in accordance with the international standard, namely the ICH guideline. In a recent publication, the ICH reference drug exposure data were used to validate a zebrafish developmental defects assay, revealing its robust performance as well as some limitations ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). Such in vivo tests with non-mammalian species together with in vitro platforms of pluripotent stem cells, namely the gastruloid assay, may be incorporated into an ensemble of NAMs to substantially reduce conventional in vivo studies using mammalian species. The present study used an 8-fold concentration margin as the threshold to evaluate whether the sensitivity of the gastruloid assay matched the in vivo data. This threshold is similar to the one used in the recent validation study of the zebrafish assay, which applied a 10-fold (1-log) concentration margin ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). Notably, even if a 4-fold concentration margin is used in the present study, only fluconazole would be reclassified as unmatch, and 17 out of the 24 drugs (70.8%) would match the in vivo. If a 2-fold margin is applied instead, 15 reference drugs (62.5%) are still classified as match. Currently, there is no consensus or criteria regarding the concentration margin or how to compare the dosimetric data of in vitro assays to the in vivo plasma concentrations for the purpose of NAM validation. This is partly due to the considerable diversity in the test format among assays and significant differences in pharmacokinetics between in vivo and in vitro. Nonetheless, it would be crucial to establish standardized procedures on how to extrapolate and compare in vitro data with the in vivo information. Such standards should help highlight the applicability and limitation of each NAM. The ICH guideline provides the in vivo drug concentrations as C max and AUC. Theoretically, C max is similar to C ave (=AUC/24) when drugs are stable and have a long half-life. By contrast, C ave is substantially lower than C max for drugs with a short half-life. The present study showed that the sensitivity of the gastruloid assay was closer to C max for some drugs, whereas it was closer to C ave for others (e.g., cytarabine, fluorouracil, hydroxyurea). Although further investigations are needed to elucidate the reasons for such differences, the mechanisms of drug actions are likely to play crucial roles. For example, cytarabine, fluorouracil, and hydroxyurea have a very short half-life, and act as chemotherapy agents by inhibiting DNA replication. For these drugs to exert robust therapeutic or toxic actions, the duration of exposure should be critical. Accordingly, C ave rather than C max (the latter persists only for a short period) may be more reflective of the adverse effect concentrations. On the other hand, for drugs that irreversibly disrupt crucial developmental processes even with a limited duration of exposure, C max may be a better indicator of the adverse effect concentrations. The present study suggests that the gastruloid assay is not sensitive enough to detect 4 ICH reference drugs, namely ribavirin, tacrolimus, thalidomide, and pomalidomide. For ribavirin, the in vitro LOAEL was 16 μM, which is markedly higher than the in vivo LOAEL of 0.052 μM (C max ) and 0.0047 μM (C ave ) in rats. Ribavirin is a nucleoside analog used as an antiviral medication in human, and its therapeutic plasma concentrations are around 10 μM ( Morello et al., 2008 ; Slavenburg et al., 2011 ). A recent study of a human pregnancy registry shows that ribavirin is unlikely to be teratogenic ( Sinclair 2022 ), suggesting that ribavirin at 10 μM is not harmful to human embryos. Also, in the zebrafish assay, the concentration of ribavirin that causes morphological alterations in 50% of the test organisms (i.e., EC50) is higher than 8 μM ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). Thus, it appears that ribavirin exerts embryotoxicity at extremely low concentrations specifically in rats. Although the embryotoxic mechanisms are unknown, there may be pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic properties unique to rats that exacerbate the adverse effects of ribavirin. For tacrolimus, the in vitro LOAEL of the gastruloid assay was 20 μM, whereas the in vivo rat LOAEL was much lower, namely 0.025 μM (C max ) and 0.0036 μM (C ave ). Tacrolimus is an inhibitor of calcineurin, and is used as an immunosuppressant ( Thomson et al., 1995 ). The therapeutic plasma concentrations in human (0.012 to 0.037 μM; Böttiger et al., 1999 ; Katada et al., 2022 ) are close to the rat LOAEL, although human studies show no increased risk of congenital malformations from the tacrolimus use during pregnancy ( Nevers et al., 2014 ; Yashima et al., 2023 ). Interestingly, the ICH guideline specifically indicates that maternal toxicity is seen in both rats and rabbits with tacrolimus at LOAEL. It may be possible that tacrolimus exerts adverse reproductive effects via maternal toxicity rather than direct impact on embryos. Alternatively, the gastruloid assay may be deficient in certain embryological tissues or events that are targeted by tacrolimus. Notably, the zebrafish assay appears to be more sensitive to tacrolimus, as EC50 is 0.08 to 0.173 μM ( Weiner et al., 2024 ), which is much closer to the rat in vivo LOAEL than the gastruloid assay. The gastruloid assay was also insensitive to thalidomide and pomalidomide, as no in vitro LOAEL was found for these drugs. Thalidomide is a notorious human teratogen ( Vargesson, 2015 ), and its structural analog pomalidomide is also presumed to be teratogenic ( ICH, 2020 ). Thalidomide causes limb defects in primates, rabbits, chicks, and zebrafish, but not in rodents ( Asatsuma-Okumura et al., 2020 ; Knobloch et al., 2017 ; Newman et al., 1993 ). The teratogenic mechanisms of thalidomide involve its binding to the cereblon (CRBN) protein, a subunit of the E3 ubiquitin ligase complex ( Ito and Handa, 2020 ). Although rodents are resistant to limb deformities, thalidomide and pomalidomide cause embryo death and other abnormalities at high doses through yet unknown mechanisms, possibly independent of CRBN. Understanding of the embryo-lethal mechanisms in rodents may reveal why gastruloids were unaffected by these drugs even at the concentrations that were considerably higher than the in vivo LOAEL. In contrast to the above 4 drugs, the gastruloid assay appeared to be excessively sensitive to retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin, and tretinoin), because the in vitro NOAEL to LOAEL ranges were markedly lower than the in vivo ranges ( Figure 3 , Table 2 ). These retinoids are clinically used as dermatological agents, and exert the therapeutic actions via the activation of retinoic acid signaling ( Scheinfeld, 2006 ). Retinoic acid signaling plays crucial roles in embryonic body patterning, so that its dysregulated activation by retinoids results in congenital malformations ( Piersma et al., 2017 ). Retinoids are generally recognized as human teratogens, and are contraindicated for use during pregnancy. Note that the zebrafish assay also appears to be excessively sensitive to retinoids, as their EC50 are markedly lower than the rat LOAEL ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). The cause of the discrepancy between the rat in vivo and these alternative assays is unclear. Because retinoids are already known as human teratogens, the excessive sensitivity of the gastruloid and zebrafish assays may not seem to be of significant concern. However, it is important to investigate whether these assays are also excessively sensitive to any other chemicals, because overestimation of toxicity may deter the use of valuable pharmaceutical drugs that are practically safe when used at proper therapeutic concentrations. Interestingly, the retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin and tretinoin) appeared to exert a unique morphological impact on the 3D gastruloids, as their concentration-response curves had a biphasic shape. Specifically, they increased area and EDI at the middle concentrations, whereas they decreased these parameters at the highest concentrations ( Figure 2 ). Such biphasic impact of the retinoids may be linked to the functional property of retinoic acid as a morphogen. A morphogen generates a concentration gradient by diffusion within a tissue, and induces different cellular responses depending on concentrations ( Simsek and Özbudak, 2022 ). Various signaling molecules, including retinoic acid, are regarded as morphogens, and are involved in the patterning of tissues during embryonic development ( Durston, 2019 ; Schilling et al., 2012 ). Retinoids act similarly to retinoic acid, and are likely to induce concentration-dependent cellular responses. It is possible that retinoids promote the growth and elongation of gastruloids at the middle concentrations, whereas they suppress these cellular behaviors at the highest concentrations. Compared to the zebrafish assay, the gastruloid assay may be more suited for the detection of cytarabine and fluorouracil, both of which are pyrimidine nucleoside analogs used for chemotherapy. For cytarabine, the NOAEL to LOAEL concentration range of the gastruloid assay (0.04 to 0.08 μM) closely matched the mouse in vivo range (0.079 to 0.31 μM C ave ) ( Figure 3 ), whereas the zebrafish assay shows no adverse effect even at the highest concentration tested (50,000 μM) ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). For fluorouracil, the NOAEL to LOAEL range of the gastruloid assay (0.2 to 0.4 μM) matched the rat in vivo range (1.2 to 1.9 μM C ave ) within a 4-fold concentration margin ( Figure 3 ), while EC50 of the zebrafish assay (9,000 μM) is markedly higher ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). The apparent insensitivity of the zebrafish assay appears to be due to the limited absorption of the nucleoside analogs by zebrafish embryos ( Weiner et al., 2024 ). Generally, zebrafish embryos absorb hydrophobic compounds more efficiently than hydrophilic ones ( de Koning et al., 2015 ; Weiner et al., 2024 ), the latter of which include nucleoside analogs, such as cytarabine and fluorouracil. By contrast, mammalian cultured cells efficiently absorb nucleoside analogs through distinct nucleoside transporters in the cell membrane ( Baldwin et al., 1999 ; Choi et al., 2012 ). Various types of nucleoside analogs have been, and will continue to be, explored as chemotherapy drugs as well as antivirals and antibacterial agents ( Geraghty et al., 2021 ; Thomson and Lamont, 2019 ). Thus, further investigations are necessary to determine whether the gastruloid assay is consistently more sensitive to nucleoside analogs than the zebrafish assay. In addition to the zebrafish assay, whole embryo culture (WEC) of rodents has been explored as a method for DART assessment. WEC enables rodent embryos, dissected from pregnant females, to grow in vitro through the early stages of organogenesis ( Flick and Klug, 2006 ; Piersma et al., 2004 ). With WEC, embryos can be directly exposed to specific chemicals that are added to the culture medium in a controlled manner. Like the zebrafish assay, WEC allows the assessment of chemical impact on complex tissue and organ structures, providing valuable insights into the nature of malformations that may be induced by exposures. By contrast, most in vitro DART assays using pluripotent stem cells, including gastruloids, are simplified, so that adverse effects may not be easily translated to assess what types of developmental defects may be induced. Nonetheless, stem cell-based assays are beneficial in that they are devoid of live vertebrate animals, unlike the WEC and zebrafish assays. In light of the growing interest in animal-free toxicity testing, validated stem cell assays are valuable, particularly as the first-tier screening tools, so that the use of live animals can be significantly reduced. The present study demonstrated that 3D gastruloids of mouse pluripotent stem cells serve as effective tools for DART assessment, which could partly substitute for rodent-based in vivo tests. However, a main goal of DART assessment, whether based on in vitro or in vivo animal tests, is to predict the embryotoxicity on ‘human’ embryos. Clearly, toxicological experimentations with actual human embryos are ethically unacceptable. Accordingly, the in vitro differentiation of human pluripotent stem cells has been explored as models of human embryogenesis to investigate the embryotoxicity of various chemicals ( Niethammer et al., 2022 ; Piersma et al., 2022 ; Worley et al., 2018 ). In recent years, various types of 3D human stem cell-based models have been reported, which recapitulate distinct aspects of embryogenesis, such as preimplantation development, gastrulation, neurulation, and somitogenesis ( Arias et al., 2022 ; Heidari Khoei et al., 2023 ; Mirdass et al., 2023 ; Sozen et al., 2022 ). However, it may be difficult to rigorously validate these models as effective NAM for DART assessment, because the toxicological data, such as NOAEL and LOAEL plasma concentrations in human, are usually unavailable even for notorious human teratogens. Regardless, human stem cell-based models may provide valuable insights on the potential impact of chemical exposures that are unique to human embryos. These models, in conjunction with non-human in vitro systems (e.g., mouse gastruloid) and non-mammalian in vivo platforms (e.g., zebrafish), should substantially reduce the use of live mammals for DART assessment.
